coredata.dc:title: Platinum/carbon dots nanocomposites from palm bunch hydrothermal synthesis as highly efficient peroxidase mimics for ultra-low H2O2 sensing platform through dual mode of colorimetric and fluorescent detection

coredata.dc:description: Detection of hydrogen peroxide and glucose in nanomolar level is crucial for point-of-care medical diagnosis. It has been reported that human's central nervous system diseases such as Alzheimer's disease, Parkinson's disease, and even amyotrophic lateral sclerosis, are presumably caused H2O2 or reactive radical species (ROS). Sensing of H2O2 released from human biofluids, tissues, organ from metabolism disorder at ultra-low concentration assists for early identification of severe diabetis mellitus related to glucose, and heart attack, as well as stroke related to cholesterol. In this work, carbon dots (CDs) having an average diameter at 6.99 nm with highly photoluminescence performance were successfully synthesized from palm empty fruit bunch (EFB) using green and environmentally friendly process via hydrothermal condition. CDs acted well on peroxidase-like activity for H2O2 detection at room temperature, however their sensitivity on ultra-low H2O2 concentration needed to be improved. To enhance their reactivity on H2O2 nanozyme activity at room temperature, synthesis of hybrid metal nanoparticles (AgNPs and PtNPs) on CDs surface was established. The findings exhibited that CDs/PtNPs was the most suitable nanozyme achieving highly efficient peroxidase mimic for dual mode of colorimetric and fluorescent H2O2 sensing platform at very low limit of detection of 0.01 mM (10 nM) H2O2.

(C) The UV–Visible absorption spectra of the CDs, CDs/PtNPs and CDs/AgNPs with scanned wavelength interval from 200 to 600 nm; inset was optical band gap from Tauc Plot.

The fluorescence emission spectra of (A) CDs, (B) CDs/PtNPs, (C) CDs/AgNPs at various excitation wavelengths from 240 nm to 400 nm with an interval of 20 nm, and (D) the zeta-potential of CDs, CDs/PtNPs and CDs/AgNPs.

(A) The UV–Visible absorption spectra of peroxidase-like catalytic activity of neat CDs, CDs/AgNPs, and CDs/PtNPs on TMB oxidation when the reaction took place at 1 mM TMB, 10 mM H2O2 and 0.10 M HAc-NaAc buffer (pH = 4.0) at room temperature (25 °C) for 40 min, and (B) oxidized TMB calculated based on the extinction coefficient of TMB at 652 nm (39,000 M−1 cm−1) in 0.10 M HAc-NaAc buffer (pH = 4.0) for 40 min.

The experiments were performed in a 0.10 M HAc-NaAc buffer (pH 4.0) containing 1 mM TMB in total 3 mL solution with H2O2 concentration ranging from 1 to 500 μM catalyzed by (A) 100 μg mL−1 CDs/PtNPs, and (B) 50 μg mL−1 CDs/PtNPs at room temperature (25 °C).

The reaction conditions; 100 μg mL−1 CDs/PtNPs, 10 mM TMB, and 0.10 M HAc-NaAc buffer (pH = 4.0) in 3 mL total solution.

Fluorescent H2O2 sensing by CDs/PtNPs; (A) fluorescent spectroscopy of reaction at λEx = 360 nm containing 100 μg mL−1 CDs/PtNPs 0.01 μmol L−1 H2O2, 1 mM TMB, and 0.10 M HAc-NaAc buffer (pH = 4.0), (B) quenching of fluorescence intensity (F) at maximum emission wavelength λEm = 480 nm at λEx = 360 nm from 5 to 15 min with varying H2O2 concentrations from 0.01 to 1 μmol L−1, (C) steady state kinetic assay of CDs/PtNPs on H2O2 sensing between ΔF versus H2O2 concentration, and (D) linearization of ΔF versus H2O2 concentration.

(A) Schematic illustration of the oxidation of TMB and colourimetric detection of oxidized TMB and glucose, and (B) proposed sensing mechanism for the CDs/PtNPs nanocomposites as peroxidase-like nanozyme for TMB/H2O2 system.
Atomic composition of CDs, CDs/PtNPs, and CDs/AgNPs analyzed by XPS.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Detection of hydrogen peroxide and glucose in nanomolar level is crucial for point-of-care medical diagnosis. It has been reported that human's central nervous system diseases such as Alzheimer's disease, Parkinson's disease, and even amyotrophic lateral sclerosis, are presumably caused H2O2 or reactive radical species (ROS). Sensing of H2O2 released from human biofluids, tissues, organ from metabolism disorder at ultra-low concentration assists for early identification of severe diabetis mellitus related to glucose, and heart attack, as well as stroke related to cholesterol. In this work, carbon dots (CDs) having an average diameter at 6.99 nm with highly photoluminescence performance were successfully synthesized from palm empty fruit bunch (EFB) using green and environmentally friendly process via hydrothermal condition. CDs acted well on peroxidase-like activity for H2O2 detection at room temperature, however their sensitivity on ultra-low H2O2 concentration needed to be improved. To enhance their reactivity on H2O2 nanozyme activity at room temperature, synthesis of hybrid metal nanoparticles (AgNPs and PtNPs) on CDs surface was established. The findings exhibited that CDs/PtNPs was the most suitable nanozyme achieving highly efficient peroxidase mimic for dual mode of colorimetric and fluorescent H2O2 sensing platform at very low limit of detection of 0.01 mM (10 nM) H2O2.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Silver/carbon dots and platinum/carbon dots nanocomposite

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) as one of the most used substances that generates reactive radical species has been widely employed as an oxidizing agent in clinical, pharmaceutical, chemical, biological, environmental and food processes and industries. A trace amount of H2O2 residue caused a serious concern and provided a great consequence in health and environmental aspects. It has been reported that human's central nervous system diseases such as Parkinson's disease, Alzheimer's disease, and even amyotrophic lateral sclerosis, are involved the presence of H2O2 which further dissociates to generate reactive radical species (ROS) [1,2]. In addition, H2O2 has been known to be produced from a variety of enzymatic reactions, and thus it is an imperative signal molecule, and key indicator of oxidative stress occurred in biological system [3]. Under physiological conditions, ROS including H2O2 produced by metabolism are adequately eliminated from human body. However, the accumulation of H2O2 produced as a result of a malfunction in the body or taken in with the diet leads to the onset of neurodegenerative diseases. The generation of ROS in biological processes is a result of both alteration and their role in cellular signaling and regulatory pathways. Cells produce ROS as a controlled physiological process, nevertheless excessive ROS develops pathological and primes to oxidative stress and disease [4]. Detection of H2O2 released from human biofluids, tissues, organ e.g. eyes, kidneys, heart, from metabolism disorder assists for early diagnosis of severe diabetis mellitus related to glucose [5], heart attack, and stroke caused by blood cholesterol level [6]. Thus, a sensitive H2O2 sensing offers a prevalent approach for the effective recognition of various biomarkers at different biological states [7]. So far, plentiful analytical methods have been utilized for H2O2 sensing including titrimetry [8], chromatography [9,10], colorimetry [11–13], electrochemistry [14–16], resonance light scattering assays [17,18], and fluorimetry [19–21]. Among all the assays introduced, very low concentration of H2O2 at micromolar level could be detected by colorimetry and fluorimetry. Particularly, fluorimetry is a promising H2O2 detection method due to its simply, sensitivity, rapidity and capability in real time detection [22].

Carbon dots (CDs) have been recently found as a new class of nanomaterials (1–10 nm) with high photoluminescence (PL) efficiency, photostability, water solubility, biocompatibility, low cost, simple synthesis, and easy functionalization. CDs exhibit catalytic activity in various chemical reactions as well as biological reactions particularly peroxidase-like activity [23–25]. Thus, these make CDs more accessible to a variety of applications, for example, solar cells [26], biosensing, biomedicine [27,28], bioimaging, photocatalysis and electrocatalysis [29]. The synthesis of CDs can be achieved via pyrolysis, reflux [30], hydrothermal carbonization [31–35], chemical oxidation, electrochemical [36] and microwave irradiation [37,38] using a number of rich carbon sources as a precursor e.g. citric acid [39], plant biomass [40], corncob [41], lotus stem [42], bagasse pulp [43], cellulose [44,45], nanocrystalline cellulose [46], algae [42,47], etc. The hydrothermal/solvothermal methods are popular because these methods are fast, easily scalable, cost-effective, environmentally-friendly, and providing high quantum yield of CDs with aqueous soluble property [48,49].

For nanozyme application of CDs, Shi and colleagues firstly reported the application of CDs as peroxidase-like nanocatalysts and assessed their catalytic activity using 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) as a substrate for glucose detection [50]. Besides, the (photo-) catalytic activity, optoelectronic, and photoluminescent properties of CDs are considerably altered by surface functionalization or passivated with appropriate heteroatoms e.g. nitrogen, sulfur, boron, phosphorus, silicon [29,51] or even both linear and branched polymers e.g. polytheylene glycol [52], polyethylenediamine [53]. The heteroatoms in the nanocomposites specifically increase the electrical charge and specific surface area of the CDs, allowing them to generate more active sites for catalysis [54]. In addition, the organogenic elements and functional groups of CDs can influence their optical activities (e.g., absorption band, energy level, spectrum range of photoluminescence or PL, and quantum yield or QY), and make them biocompatible and well-dispersed in aqueous systems [55]. Owing to the high biocompatibility with living cells, real-time in vitro and in vivo analytical assays for H2O2 sensing using colorimetry and especially fluorescence imaging assay of CDs have been lately under extensive investigation.

Herein, we described a safe and straightforward approach for CDs synthesis using a hydrothermal process, as well as their application of CDs on peroxidase mimics via a TMB - H2O2 system. Dual modes of colorimetric and fluorimetric sensing of trace amount of H2O2 were reported. To enhance the sensitivity and kinetic reaction rate of nanozyme taking place at room temperature, small amount of either silver or platinum (1%mol) nanoparticles were fabricated and decorated with the CDs especially near or on their surface. Based on the surface plasmon resonance of hybrid metal nanoparticle/CDs composite, the investigation on the nanomolar level of H2O2 detection was conducted for both colorimetric and fluorimetric techniques. Kinetic parameters related to peroxidase mimetic reaction from the present study were calculated, and compared with peroxidase enzyme together with other nanozymes from recent previous works. The findings suggested prospective point-of-care assay for H2O2 sensing in nanomolar level for development of either real-time miniature sensors or fluorescence bioimaging assay.

Palm empty fruity bunch (EFB) biomass, the most abundant agricultural residue of palm oil processing [56] was provided by a palm oil mill, Chumphon Province, Thailand. p-Phenylenediamine was purchased from Alfa Aesar, USA. Sodium thiosulfate pentahydrate was purchased from Daejung, Korea. Chloroplatinic acid (H2Cl6Pt·xH2O) and sodium borohydride (NaBH4) were purchased from Acros Organics, USA. Sodium acetate and 3,3′,5,5′-Tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, Inc. Silver nitrate (AgNO3) was purchased from Pine Chemical, Thailand. Acetic acid (glacial 99.7%) and hydrogen peroxide (30–35.5%) were provided from Samchun Chemicals, Korea. All chemicals used were analytical reagent grade, obtained from commercial sources, and used without the purification process. The reaction and analytical solutions were prepared using ultrapure water purified by a water purification system (New Human UP System, Korea) with an electrical resistance of 18.3 MΩ cm (25 °C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon dots and hybrid carbon dots/metal nanoparticles composite
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs were synthesized by a hydrothermal method from EFB. First, 2.5 g milled EFB (+50/-200 mesh) was dissolved in 50 mL ultrapure water (solid:liquid ratio of 1:20), and soaked for 5 min for the wettability of EFB. Then, the EFB-water mixture was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel reactor, heated at 220 °C for 6 h, and then cooled down to room temperature by quenching in an ice bath. A dark brown solution was obtained after hydrothermal reaction collected by centrifugation at 11,000 rpm for 20 min and the large particles were again removed from the brown solution using microfiltration with a 0.22 μm filter membrane. Then, the solution was dialyzed by using Spectra/Por® Dialysis membrane (MWCO 3.5 kDa) for 48 h to remove all impurities such as furan derivatives, organic acids, and sugars [57].

To enhance and tune CD's enzyme mimetic reactivity and photoluminescence property, the synthesis of CDs/metal nanoparticles (NPs) was conducted. The nanocomposite of Pt and Ag nanoparticles onto CDs surface was fabricated after CDs synthesis. CDs/PtNPs nanocomposite was formed by adding 1 mL of 17 mM H2Cl6Pt and 0.55 g of NaBH4 as the reducing agent into 50 mL of 0.3 mg mL−1 CDs aqueous solution and vigorously stirred at room temperature (25 °C) for 48 h. The solution was suddenly turned from light to dark brown, indicating that the CDs/PtNPs nanocomposites were formed. The nanocomposite of CDs and Ag nanoparticles was synthesized using similar method by reducing CDs and silver nitrate with NaBH4 and the final material was named as CDs/AgNPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CDs and hybrid CD/metal nanoparticles composite
ce:section.ce:para:
Fluorescence measurement of CDs and CDs/metal nanoparticles composite was performed by a Model FP-6200 Spectrofluorometer (PerkinElmer, Japan) with a scan speed of 500 nm min−1 with an excitation and emission slit width of 10 nm. The fluorescence behaviour was studied by varying an excitation wavelength in a range of 240–400 nm with a 20 nm increment. The QY of the CDs and CDs/metal nanocomposites was determined using Equation (1) when the reference is quinine sulfate (QY = 54%) dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4, I is the integrated intensity area of fluorescent spectra, A is the UV–vis absorbance at the specific excitation wavelength yielding maximum photoluminescent emission intensity, and η is the refractive index of the solvent [58].

The morphological characterization was analyzed by JEM-2100 Plus High-resolution Transmission Electron Microscope (HRTEM) (JEOL, Japan) operated at 200 kV. The UV-1800 UV–Vis double beam spectrophotometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Japan) was used to record the UV–vis absorption of the synthesized CDs and CDs/metal nanoparticles composite with the wavelength range between 200 and 600 nm. Zeta potential measurement was conducted triplicates for each sample by a nanoPartica SZ-100V2 Series (Malvern Panalytical, Japan). The surface elemental composition of samples was characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (PHI5000 VersaProbe II, ULVAC-PHI, Japan) at the SUT-NANOTEC-SLRI XAS beamline (BL5.2) of the Synchrotron Light Research Institute (Public Organization), Thailand. The samples were excited by a monochromatized Al-Kα X-ray source (hγ = 1486.6 eV) and PHI MultiPak XPS software was applied to fit XPS spectra with a combination of Gaussian–Lorentzian functions for the best fited curve. The Shirley-type background was used to remove the background from the spectrum before the peak fitting. The survey spectra were recorded with 1.000 eV energy step with a pass energy of 117.4 eV, meanwhile, the high-resolution spectra were recorded with 0.05 eV energy step with a pass energy of 46.95 eV.

The morphology and internal structure of all samples and chemical elements were characterized by a field emission transmission electron microscope (FETEM, Model JEM-3100F, JEOL, Japan) at the accelerating voltage of 300 kV and energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) (Oxford Instruments) at 0–20 keV. The crystal plane analysis of the samples was analyzed by X-ray diffractometry (XRD) (D2 PHASER, Bruker, Germany) with 2theta between 10 and 70° and an increment of 0.02°.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Screening of peroxidase-like activity of CDs, CDs/PtNPs, and CDs/AgNPs for colorimetric H2O2 detection
ce:section.ce:para:
For colorimetric analytical assay for H2O2 detection, the peroxidase-like reaction mixture contained 100 μg mL−1 of nanozyme (CDs, CDs/AgNPs, or CDs/PtNPs), 400 μL of 1.0 mM TMB, and an exact volume of 0.1 M NaAc-Hac buffer (pH 4.0) to 3.0 mL final volume. Finally, H2O2 was added into the mixture to the final concentration of 1 mmol L−1. The reaction mixture was shaked vigorously at 250 rpm and incubated at 25 °C for 40 min. The UV–vis absorbance of the solution after 40 min was measured at 652 nm for the determination of the oxidized TMB. The amount of oxidized TMB concentration when using different nanozyms was calculated from the absorbance at 652 nm according to the Beer Lambert's law as shown in Equation (2).
(2) A = εTMB × C × L
When A is absorbance at 652 nm for oxidized TMB having the molar extinction coefficients of 39000 M−1 cm−1, L is the part length (cm), and C is the oxidized TMB concentration (mol L−1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Kinetic study on peroxidase-like activity at various H2O2 concentrations
ce:section.ce:para:
The peroxidase-like activities of selected nanozymes from the screening study (CDs and CDs/PtNPs) were studied on peroxidase mimetic activity towards TMB oxidation. Briefly, 100 μg mL−1 CDs/PtNPs or CDs was added into 0.10 M NaAc-Hac buffer solution (pH 4.0) at temperature 25 °C, and followed by the addition of 400 μL of 1 mmol L−1 TMB. The concentration of H2O2 was varied from 0.1 to 1000 μM in the final mixture of 3 mL. Subsequently, the reaction solutions were incubated at 25 °C and the UV–Vis spectroscopy was carried out. The absorbance in a range of 200 nm and 700 nm was recorded from 0 to 40 min. The velocity of oxidized TMB formation during the reaction was monitored by measuring the change in UV–Vis absorbance at 652 nm. The initial velocity of the reaction was calculated and then plotted against TMB or H2O2 concentration. The maximum velocity of oxidized TMB formation during the time course of reaction (vmax), and the Michaelis−Menten constant, Km, denoting the affinity of the nanozyme mimicking peroxidase toward the substrate which is H2O2 in this case, were calculated from a Lineweaver−Burk plot according to Equation (3), and Equation (4). For the kinetic study using TMB as limiting substrate, TMB concentration was varied from 0.1 μM to 700 μM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of CDs, CDs/PtNPs and CDs/AgNPs
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs was synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal process of EFB in ultrapure water at 220 °C for 6 h. The CDs/PtNPs and CDs/AgNPs nanocomposites were prepared by reduction of Pt nanoparticles (PtNPs) or Ag nanoparticles (AgNPs) within CDs using NaBH4 as a reducing agent at room temperature (25 °C) for 48 h. Fig. 1 (A) showed that the aqueous solution of the (a) CDs under the day light was light brown, while the (b) CDs/PtNPs, and (c) CDs/AgNPs solutions were light gray-brown colour and burnt orange colour, respectively. The green emitting fluorescence was detected under the 366 nm for both CDs and CDs/PtNPs, whereas a diminutive reduction of fluorescence property was found for CDs/AgNPs (Fig. 1(B)). The UV–Vis absorption spectra of aqueous solution of CDs, CDs/PtNPs and CDs/AgNPs were shown in Fig. 1(C). The UV–Vis absorption peak of CDs was exhibited at 277 nm, while a shoulder of both CDs/PtNPs and CDs/AgNPs was found at the same absorption peak at 258 nm. This indicated that the π- π* transition of aromatic sp2 domain of the CC conjugated bond of CDs was occurred. It was reported that the absorption peak less than 300 nm, especially between 240 and 265 nm was presumably assigned to the π → π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains [59] of the conjugated CC bonds associated with a carbon core (carbogenic core) [60]. The result was in good agreement with previous research on Pt-CDs hybrid nanoparticles [61]. Slightly observed peak at 350 nm was found in case of CDs/AgNPs indicating the existence of n-π* transitions from CO bonds [62,63].

The fluorescence property of CDs, CDs/PtNPs and CDs/AgNPs was demonstrated in Fig. 2 . The neat CDs (Fig. 2(A)) showed the highest fluorescence emission intensity at 425 nm (λem = 425 nm) with excitation wavelength 340 nm (λex = 340 nm). The excitation-wavelength-independent characteristic was found for CDs with varying the excitation from 240 nm to 400 nm. The CDs/PtNPs and CDs/AgNPs showed the excitation-wavelength-dependent property in the range from 350 nm to 500 nm (Fig. 2(B) and (C)). The CDs/PtNPs and CDs/AgNPs displayed the λem = 475 nm and 485 nm with λex = 360 nm and 280 nm, respectively. CDs/AgNPs and CDs/PtNPs exhibited double peaks of emission due to the alteration of surface resonance of CDs when Ag and Pt nanoparticles were grown on CDs surface. It has been revealed that fabrication of CDs nanocomposites with metal elements was conducted to alter their molecular absorption and emission wavelength providing the red-shift phenomenon, to gain a particular reactivity, and to improve the selectivity towards a specific target [64]. The red-shift phenomenon was markedly observed because of the metal impregnation on surface of CDs pristine. In good accordance, the CDs/metal nanocomposite, CDs/PtNPs and CDs/AgNPs, demonstrated the effect on decreasing of the optical band gap energy of CDs as shown in Fig. 1(C) indicating the emission ability at longer excitation wavelength. All together with red-shift phenomenon, synthesis of Pt and Ag nanocomposite on CDs surface caused a significant decrease in optical band gap from 5.49 eV for neat CDs to 4.25 eV and 4.15 eV for CDs/AgNPs and CDs/PtNPs, respectively (Fig. 1(C)). This contributes to surface plasmon resonance of the CDs/AgNPs and CDs/PtNPs nanocomposite [65] owing to the free transfer of electrons between relatively closer conduction and valence bands in the CDs/AgNPs and CDs/PtNPs nanocomposites. Due to the combined oscillation of the electrons in resonance with the light wave, freely available electrons in the CDs/AgNPs and CDs/PtNPs generated red shift emission band [66]. Although, CDs/AgNPs and CDs/PtNPs provided tuneable ability of red-shift fluorescence emission, slightly reduction of fluorescence intensity was obtained relative to CDs (Fig. 2(A)-2(C)). As a result, QYs of CDs, CDs/AgNPs and CDs/PtNPs were 39%, 24%, and 33%, respectively.

Regarding the change of zeta potential (Fig. 2(D)), the results demonstrated that PtNPs and AgNPs could be successfully loaded on the surface of the CDs through electrostatic interaction between the negative charges of CDs and the positive charges of PtNPs and AgNPs. Fig. 2(D) shows that the zeta potential of unconjugated CDs, CDs/PtNPs and CDs/AgNPs were −24.8, −23.7 and −14.0 mV, respectively demonstrating that all samples have highly negative charges on their surfaces. The greatly negative surface potential obtained could be attributed to the deprotonation of –COOH, and –OH on the surface of CDs. It has been reported that the negative zeta potential is because of dense electron cloud which concentrates on the surface of CDs [67]. Pt and Ag nanoparticles grown on CDs surface caused a slightly reduction of negative surface charges for CDs/PtNPs and CDs/AgNPs in a respective degree indicated by a decrease of zeta potential representing a complete coverage of PtNPs and AgNPs on CDs surface [61,68]. Less negatively zeta potential of CDs/PtNPs and CDs/AgNPs compared with CDs indicated that positive charged metallic Pt0, Pt2+ and Pt4+ as well as Ag0, Ag+ and Ag2+ from the reduction reaction reside within the CDs, particulary on or near the CDs surface. This resulted in a shift of zeta potential value from highly negative to more neutral value (0 mV), or even highly positive zeta potential due to metal ions adsorbed on CDs. Similar findings were reported for metal doped CDs and neat CDs for detection of metal ions application [51,67,69]. Still, highly negative zeta potential of all CDs in the present study namely CDs, CDs/PtNPs and CDs/AgNPs signified high stability of nanoparticles in aqueous phase and further demonstrated an effective binding with cations such as heavy metal ions [67], amino group and positive charged molecules, therefore presumably suitable to use as peroxidase mimetic nanozyme with least possibility to hydroxide anion group or –OH and hydroxyl radicals or HO⋅ scavenger.

There were evidences that the optical band gap increases with the decrease in the size of the CDs, and the smaller size CDs were able to be excited at a lower wavelength. However, from HRTEM image analysis, the size of all CDs, CDs/PtNPs and CDs/AgNPs is uniform between an average diameter from 1.93 to 6.99 nm (Fig. 3 ), therefore the metal nanoparticles conjugated on CDs’ surface state otherwise play a vital role over the CDs size on the quantum confinement characteristic. This is noted that PtNPs and AgNPs on CDs surface substantially influenced the tunable optical band gap and photonic properties of CDs.

The morphology of CDs, CDs/PtNPs and CDs/AgNPs characterized by HRTEM was presented in Fig. 3. HRTEM images and the corresponding dot size distribution of CDs, CDs/PtNPs and CDs/AgNPs show that all samples are well-dispersed with the average particles size between 1 and 12 nm with spherical geometry. The CDs showed the average particle size of 6.99 ± 1.69 nm which were greater than CDs/PtNPs (1.93 ± 0.34 nm) and CDs/AgNPs (4.94 ± 1.47 nm). The lattice d-spacing of CDs, CDs/PtNPs and CDs/AgNPs were 0.34 nm, 0.38 nm and 0.27 nm, respectively corresponding to the (100) plane lattice of graphite [70,71]. The greater d-spacing refers to more amorphous structure. The graphitized and amorphous carbon structures of CDs, CDs/PtNPs and CDs/AgNPs were also identified by XRD pattern shown in Fig. 4 .

Fig. 4 shows the XRD patterns of the CDs, CDs/PtNPs, and CDs/AgNPs. A broad hump between 2θ = 15–35° in the XRD pattern represented amorphous moieties of carbon structure in CDs. Several intense sharp peaks at 2θ = 11.3°, and 20.9° demonstrating the polycrystalline characteristics of CDs [72]. The CDs showed a small sharp peak at 2θ = 20.9° attributed to (002) plane of the sp3 graphitic carbon core [72] which corresponded to an interplanar d-spacing of 0.347 nm (3.47 Å) in HRTEM image shown in Fig. 3(A1). The d-spacing value obtained in the present study was larger than that of bulk graphite (3.34 Å) [73]. In case of CDs/PtNPs, relatively sharp peaks at 2θ = 40.1°, 45.1°, and 67.1° were assigned to the (111), (200), and (220) planes of the face-centred cubic (fcc) structure of the Pt nanoparticles [74,75], whereas the XRD diffraction pattern of CDs/AgNPs accompanied peak intensity at 38.3°, 44.8°, 64.7°, and 77.7° corresponding to the (111), (200), (220), and (311) reflection planes of Ag nanoparticles, respectively [76,77].

The XPS analysis of CDs, CDs/PtNPs and CDs/AgNPs surface state was shown in Fig. 5 . The full-scan spectra of CDs, CDs/PtNPs and CDs/AgNPs were mainly composed of C and O elements at binding energy 285 eV for C1s, and 531 eV for O1s (Fig. 5(A1)). In case of CDs/PtNPs and CDs/AgNPs, Pt and Ag were observed at binding energy around 72–77 eV for Pt4f [78] and 368–375 eV for Ag3d [79–81], respectively (Fig. 5(B1) and 5(C1)). It is worth noting that the %atomic content of Pt and Ag was lower than 6% (slightly less than 1 mol% metal loading) from EDS (Fig. S1) results, therefore Pt 4f and Ag 3d surface state XPS peaks were less intense compared with C1s and O1s.

The high resolution of C1s peaks showed three XPS peaks at 284.6, 286.8 and 288.7 eV assigned to C–C/CC, C–O and O–CO respectively which was mainly found in CDs. In case of CDs/PtNPs and CDs/AgNPs, XPS patterns exhibited primarily C1s peak position at 284.6 eV but small peaks were observed at 286.8 and 288.7 eV contributed to C–O and O–CO bonds (Fig. 5(B2) and 5(C2)), respectively. Similarly, the O1s occurrence of CDs was found in large three peaks at 531.0, 531.7 and 532.8 eV corresponding to CO, C–O–C and O–CO bonds, in case of Pt doped CDs, smaller peak area of all three binding energies was observed (Fig. 5(A3) and 5(B3)). In contrast, O1s peaks of CDs/AgNPs revealed only two peaks at 531.0 and 532.8 eV ascribed to CO and O–CO bonds. The XPS analysis results confirmed promising thorough reduction reaction during synthesis of hybrid Ag and Pt within CDs. The atomic percentage of each bonding was quantified using integrated peak area data as shown in Table 1.

As demonstrated in Fig. 5(B4), the existence of metallic Pt0 was mainly observed as ascribed at the peaks of 71.6 eV and 74.9 eV which could be obtained due to the oxygen reduction reaction during nanocomposition fabrication, while PtO was less intense demonstrated at the peak at 72.6 eV [78]. From Fig. 5(C4), high resolution XPS analysis of Ag exhibited two additional signals of CDs/AgNPs. The results revealed that the Ag3d peaks comprise Ag 3d3/2 at 373.9 eV and Ag 3d5/2 at 367.9 eV [83] which indicates that Ag occurs in metallic Ag0 form. The presence of Pt and Ag on the surface of CDs at 0.17% and 5.69% atomic, respectively was confirmed by the EDS analysis (Fig. S1).

Based on our results, the CDs/PtNPs and CDs/AgNPs could considerably narrow the optical bandgap energy compared to CDs pristine (Fig. 1(C)). Moreover, CDs/PtNPs and CDs/AgNPs possessed ability on tunable red-shift fluorescence emission, high dispersity and high stability based on zeta potential with negatively charged surface state. Therefore, the reactivity of CDs/PtNPs and CDs/AgNPs on peroxidase-like activity was further investigated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric sensing on peroxidase-like activity of CDs, CDs/PtNPs and CDs/AgNPs
ce:section.ce:para:
The peroxidase mimic activity of CDs, CDs/PtNPs and CDs/AgNPs was identified by the ability toward hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) dissociation to generate hydroxide anions [84], hydroxyl radicals (⋅OH) and trace amount of reactive oxygen species (ROS) which led to TMB oxidation [85]. This assay plays a very beneficial role on small molecule detection in biological processes such as glucose, cholesterol, and lactate. In case of glucose detection, H2O2 is generated from enzymatic reaction catalyzed by glucose oxidase (GOx) and thus H2O2 sensing is an indirect assay for glucose monitoring catalyzed by horseradish peroxidase (HRP) according to Scheme 1 (A) as shown in equations (5) and (6), respectively.
(5)
D − glucose +
H 2

→
G
O x
G l u c o n i c
a c i d +

(6)
H 2

H R P

B
( o x )

Since the reaction took place at room temperature of 25 °C, therefore the ability of CDs, CDs/PtNPs and CDs/AgNPs as nanozyme to enhance catalytic reactivity of TMB oxidation by H2O2 was tested. The results from Fig. 6 (A) and 6(B) demonstrated that CDs/PtNPs, and neat CDs substantially catalyzed the hydrogen peroxide dissociation to OH- and ⋅OH radicals at 25 °C which subsequently catalyzed TMB oxidation to form oxidized TMB with blue color detected at 652 nm. The amount of oxidized TMB from CDs/PtNPs and CDs were 43.5 mol% and 38.5 mol%, respectively (Fig. 6(B)). Besides, CDs/PtNPs demonstrated superior reaction velocity compared with CDs as shown in Fig. S1.

The peroxidase catalytic oxidation of TMB with H2O2 encompasses two steps of single electron transfer process. Firstly, TMB is oxidized by a single electron to obtain a TMB radical cation. Two molecules of TMB intermediate radical cations subsequently form a complex having blue color absorbed the UV–Vis irradiation at λ = 370 nm and λ = 652 nm. Further oxidation via the second step single electron transfer could generate 3,3,5,5-tetramethylbenzidine diimine (TMBDI) having the absorbance at λ = 450 nm [86]. In the present work, the strong oxidation of TMB by CDs/PtNPs and CDs gave substantially high absorbance at all three absorption bands of TMB oxidation products at 652 nm, 370 nm and 450 nm as shown in Fig. 6(A). Amount of oxidized TMB was simply quantified by absorbance at λ = 652 nm as demonstrated in Fig. 6(B). It has been proposed that the catalytic pathway of CDs on hydrogen peroxide dissociation follows a ping-pong mechanism via one-electron transfer reaction similar to peroxidases (PODs) mechanism [29]. Once H2O2 reacts with CDs, then CDs* is formed, and spontaneously returned to CDs while reactive oxygen species (ROS) are simultaneously generated [50].

According to a previous report on mechanism of H2O2 dissociation in acidic and basic condition at 30 °C, the activation energies of various metals on the rate-determining steps of peroxidase-like activity for H2O2 dissociation were arranged in the order of Au(111) > Ag(111) > Pt(111) > Pd(111) at both acidic and basic conditions [87]. However, the adsorption affinity was ordered as Au < Ag < Pt < Pd for H2O2 on their surfaces, respectively [88–90]. Therefore, the results of adsorption energy of Ag toward H2O2 molecule (−0.16 eV) was slightly lower than that of Pt (−0.26 eV), whereas the activation energy barriers of rate-limiting step of Pt (0.18 eV) was 2.3 times lower than that of Ag (0.41 eV). In the present work, the lower activation energy of Pt exhibited greater influence on enhanced reactivity of the H2O2 dissociation, eventhough the adsorption energy between metals and H2O2 are also indicator for descriptive explanation toward enzyme mimetic activity that have been advocated for the further catalytic activity of metals [91,92].

The structure of CDs has been reported to influence the catalytic reaction of H2O2 dissociation. The CDs containing graphitic carbon nanocrystalline core identified by sp2 carbon atom with carboxylic/carbonyl functionality at the edge is considerably enhancing the reduction reaction of H2O2 [93,94]. The high ratio of sp2/sp3 of CDs and CDs/PtNPs relative to other nanozymes for crystalline to amorphous morphology of carbon core was confirmed by XPS and XRD data as shown in Figs. 4 and 5, respectively. The results are in good agreement with a study on density functional theory (DFT) stating that graphene surface having carboxylic groups increases the reactivity for H2O2 dissociation reaction [95]. It is noted that the –CO groups advocated as the catalytically active sites toward H2O2, in the interim OC–O- and the –OH groups on CDs surface serve independently as the binding sites for the substrate, and suppress side-product from catalytic activity [96]. The existence of carbonyl, hydroxyl and carboxyl groups of CDs and CDs/PtNPs was confirmed by XPS analysis in Fig. 5 and Table 1. The high degree of ketonic carbonyl groups of CDs and CDs/PtNPs is promising to facilitate the reduction of H2O2. In contrast, CDs/AgNPs in this work demonstrated an adverse effect on H2O2 reduction and thus produced low level of TMB oxidation. The catalytic mechanism of TMB oxidation is due to an increase in the electron density and mobility in the CDs by reason of the electron transfer from TMB to CDs [50]. Therefore, the CDs nanocomposite with PtNPs heteroatoms was found to give higher catalytic reactability mainly owing to their favorably strong affinity toward the substrate which is H2O2 with the higher electron transfer rate. CDs/PtNPs presumably facilitated the electron shuttle and thus enhanced the rate of H2O2 dissociation to OH- and ⋅OH compared with neat CDs without Pt nanoparticles composite as demonstrated in Scheme 1(B).

To study the sensitivity of the peroxidase-like reaction from different CDs/PtNPs loadings on colorimetric sensing of H2O2, the UV–Vis absorbance of oxidized TMB was monitored at 652 nm during 0–20 min at room temperature (25 °C). As presented in Fig. 7 , effect of high CDs/PtNPs loading at 100 μg mL−1 (Fig. 7(A)), and lower CDs/PtNPs loading at 50 μg mL−1 on TMB oxidation were investigated. Different concentrations of H2O2 were tested for each concentration of CDs/PtNPs in the H2O2 dissociation reaction. The results demonstrated that high concentration of CDs/PtNPs at 100 μg mL−1 caused an instantaneous increase of oxidized TMB only until 5 min and 10 min when high H2O2 concentration were used at 500 and 100 μM, respectively. Afterward, the amount of oxidized TMB reached constant value as shown in Fig. 7(A). This is presumably because at high CDs/PtNPs loading could lead to rapid dissociation of H2O2 and in system with too high H2O2 concentration can cause too much generation of OH-, ⋅OH radicals as well as ROS which led to self-dissociation of H2O2 without TMB oxidation. In contrast, at low concentration of H2O2 (1, 5 and 20 μM) exhibited higher sensitivity of the TMB oxidation reaction (Fig. 7(A)). And thus at 100 μg mL−1 CDs/PtNPs loading was most favorable to react with 5 μM H2O2 with highest reaction velocity calculated from the change in UV–Vis absorbance over the reaction time.

At lower CDs/PtNPs loading of 50 μg mL−1 (Fig. 7(B)), much slower rate of TMB oxidation was obviously observed at the same H2O2 concentration. For example, in the presence of 5 μM H2O2 and 5 min of TMB oxidation, the reaction velocities approximated by ΔAbs/Δt were 0.057 a.u. min−1 from 50 μg mL−1 CDs/PtNPs loading (Figs. 7(B)), and 0.109 a.u. min−1 from 100 μg mL−1 CDs/PtNPs loading (Fig. 7(A)). Consequently, an increase nanozyme loading could substantially enhance H2O2 dissociation for TMB oxidation at low H2O2 concentration. Therefore, a suitable CDs/PtNPs loading of 100 μg mL−1 was used for the further experiment on ultra-low H2O2 colorimetric detection due to higher sensitivity of the reaction.

The catalytic activity of the substrate was also dependent on many factors, such as the pH of solution [87,97], temperature [98], metal nanoparicles size [99], and metal loading [100,101]. Accordingly, the reaction kinetics of peroxidase mimetic of CDs/PtNPs were investigated for the H2O2 concentration varied from 0.1 to 1000 μM. Fig. 8 (A) showed an increase of the UV–Vis absorbance at 652 nm of oxidized TMB catalyzed by 100 μg mL−1 CDs/PtNPs at 10 μM H2O2 with an increase of reaction duration from 1 to 50 min at room temperature (25 °C). The findings demonstrated that the amount of oxidized TMB expressed as the UV–Vis absorbance at 652 nm enlarged with increasing H2O2 concentration as shown in Fig. 8(B). Apart from that, no suppression was observed for the CDs/PtNP-catalyzed process at H2O2 concentration as low as 0.1 μM, indicating that CDs/PtNPs had sustained catalytic activity at ultra-low H2O2 concentrations. Nevertheless, no significant difference of oxidized TMB absorbance change between 0.1 and 0.5 μM of H2O2, therefore the limit of colorimetric detection (LOD) of H2O2 in this CDs/PtNPs-TMB-H2O2 system was 0.5 μM. After monitoring the alteration of oxidized TMB between 500 and 1000 μM during the reaction period up to 30 min, the results revealed that maximum H2O2 detection for this system was at 1000 μM. From Fig. 8(C), the kinetic study was performed within initial state of reaction from 0 to 7 min, it was found that the velocity of the reaction was varied based on the initial H2O2 concentration which was limiting substrate in this case. As a result, the Lineweaver−Burk plot using double reciprocal of reaction velocity (v) and substrate concentration [S] which was H2O2 provided a linear correlation of these two sets of data illustrated in Fig. 8(D), and the linear regression analysis (inset of Fig. 8(D)) informed that the linear range of H2O2 detection of the proposed TMB/H2O2 system catalyzed by CDs/PtNPs was in the range of 10 μM–500 μM H2O2 with R2 = 0.992. Photographs of reaction solution under visible light and UV excitation at 366 nm was demonstrated in Fig. S2.

The steady-state kinetic assay was used to evaluate the parameters of peroxidase mimicking performance of CDs/PtNPs based on the aforementioned substrate concentration-dependent catalytic activity. When H2O2 was the limiting substrate varying from 0.1 μM to 1000 μM in the presence of excess TMB at 10 mM, typical Michaelis– Menten profile was obtained similar to peroxidase activity as shown in Fig. 8(D), and the steady-state kinetic parameters namely the maximum reaction velocity, Vmax, and Michaelis constant, Km, were calculated using Lineweaver–Burk plots (inset of Fig. 8(D)). The Km and Vmax of various types of nanozyme from the previous works were compared with the present study in Table 2 . The Km value has been used as an indicator of enzyme affinity toward the substrates, thus a high affinity between nanozyme and H2O2 is represented by a low Km, and vice versa. In the present work, apparent Km of CDs/PtNPs toward H2O2 substrate was 0.216 mmol L−1 which was relatively lower than HRP (3.702 mmol L−1) [102], Fe-CD (0.58 mmol L−1) [103], N-CQD (0.4913 mmol L−1) [104] and C42H118Bi2Cu7N28Na4O97W18 compound (0.25 mmol L−1) [105]. This indicated the higher affinity of CDs/PtNPs onto H2O2 compared with other nanozymes aforementioned. Moreover, Vmax at 1.766 × 10−8 mol L−1 s−1 of the proposed TMB/H2O2 system catalyzed by 100 μg mL−1 CDs/PtNPs was comparable with other works, and even much greater than that catalyzed by Pt NPs (3.3 × 10−11 mol L−1 s−1) [106], and Fe3O4@C (1.799 × 10−9 mol L−1 s−1) [107].

In case of TMB as the limiting substrate, Km of CDs/PtNPs toward TMB substrate was 0.255 mmol L−1 (Fig. S3) which was lower than HRP (0.434 mmol L−1) [102], Fe3O4@C (0.38 mmol L−1) [107], and similar to AgNPs (0.25 mmol L−1) [109] as shown in Table 2. Photographs of reaction solution with varying TMB concentration under visible light and UV excitation at 366 nm was demonstrated in Fig. S4. In addition, Vmax of the reaction catalyzed by CDs/PtNPs toward TMB substrate was 8.077 × 10−7 mol L−1 s−1 which was much greater than HRP (1.24 × 10−87 mol L−1 s−1) [102], C42H118Bi2Cu7N28Na4O97W18 compound (5.25 × 10−8 mol L−1 s−1) [105], Fe-CDs (5.97 × 10−8 mol L−1 s−1) [103], Ag NPs (3.3 × 10−7 mol L−1 s−1) [109], Pt NPs (3.30 × 10−7 mol L−1 s−1) [106], AuNPs@CDs (1.86 × 10−8 mol L−1 s−1) [24], N-rGO (3.52 × 10−8 mol L−1 s−1) [110], and similar degree as Fe3O4@C (7.399 × 10−7 mol L−1 s−1) [107]. The findings suggested a stronger affinity of CDs/PtNPs onto TMB molecules compared with other nanozymes recently reported. The reason was mainly resulted from the electrostatic attraction between suitable amount of carboxyl groups on CDs/PtNPs surface and amino group of TMB [115]. Afterward, TMB molecules adsorbed on the surfaces of CDs/PtNPs donated lone-pair electrons from the amine groups to the CDs/PtNPs, increasing the electron density and mobility in the CDs/PtNPs, which further accelerated the electron transfer from CDs/PtNPs to H2O2, resulting in the reduction of H2O2 to H2O.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Turn-off fluorescent sensing mode on peroxidase-like activity of CDs/PtNPs
ce:section.ce:para: As H2O2 is an important intermediate found in biological system in a very trace amount, an improvement of sensitivity and limit of detection of H2O2 are of great necessity and broad interest. It has been investigated that fluorimetric sensing of nanozymes having fluorescence characteristics facilitates the sensitive assay and lower the limit of detection (LOD) of substrates. In the present work, the developed H2O2 fluorimetric sensing assay was turn-off fluorescence mode for CDs/PtNPs nanozyme which was presumably based on the catalytic growth of PtNPs on CDs surface [24]. The results from colorimetric assay indicated that CDs/PtNPs showed a superior synergistic effect between the CDs and PtNPs on H2O2 dissociation and thus increasing the amount of oxidized TMB identified by an augment of UV–Vis absorbance at 652 nm. In contrast to colorimetric assay, the fluorescence of CDs/PtNPs was turned off owing to the reduction of H2O2 which presumably caused an enhanced interaction of Pt ions with carboxyl, amine, and other functional groups on the surface of CDs which led to surface plasmon-enhanced energy transfer from CDs (donor) to PtNPs (acceptor). Consequently, the fluorescence of CDs is efficiently suppressed as shown in Fig. 9 (A). There was an evidence that the destruction of the CDs/AgNPs nano-complex structure by H2O2 could cause the fate fluorescence regarding the peroxidase-like reaction. Accordingly, the restoration of CDs/PtNPs fluorescence, along with the turn-on detection of fluorescence signal is potentially applied for analyte quantitative analysis such as dopamine, ascorbic acid, etc [116].

As demonstrated in Fig. 9(A), an increase of reaction duration gradually decreased the fluorescence intensity of CDs/PtNPs even at very low H2O2 concentration of 0.01 μM while the control experiment without H2O2 indicated no obvious effect on their fluorescence performance. When varying H2O2 concentrations between 0.01 μM and 1.0 μM, Fig. 9(B) demonstrated that the rate of fluorescence intensity reduction from all H2O2 concentrations which corresponded to reaction velocity was similar between the reaction time from 5 to 15 min which represented the ping pong effect of CDs/PtNPs as peroxidase-like activity. The steady-state kinetic assay was employed to determine the detection performance when H2O2 was the limiting substrate (Fig. 9(C)). As shown in Table 2, this method allowed the H2O2 detection in a range of 1.0–100 × 10−8 M or 10 nM–1000 nM with a detection limit as low as 1.0 × 10−8 M or 10 nM. It is remarkable that the proposed assay achieved a considerably lower LOD than the previously aforementioned colorimetric assay. Moreover, the present study on CDs/PtNPs based fluorescent H2O2 sensing assay offered more sensitive response than other recently published works both colorimetric and fluorescent spectroscopic methods for H2O2 detection in micro-nano molar level [112,114,117,118].

CDs having an average diameter below 10 nm were successfully synthesized from palm EFB as a renewable source via hydrothermal reaction in a one-step process. The synthesized CDs showed excellent photoluminescence property with tunable emission wavelength from nanocomposite fabrication with metal nanoparticles. At room temperature, synthesis of hybrid CDs/PtNPs nanocomposite exhibited most outstanding peroxidase-like activity and catalyzed the oxidation of TMB by H2O2. Highly sensitive H2O2 sensing at ultra-low amount in nanomolar level was effectively done for dual mode of colorimetric and fluorimetric assays. The proposed method potentially further implement for indirectly determining glucose in human body fluids containing very low level of glucose concentration such as tear, sweat and saliva. The findings demonstrating the prospective point-of-care assay for implanted miniature biosensor and fluorescence bioimaging applications.
